md: ### The tangent line at a point
A line that touches a circle at exactly one point — without crossing it — is called a **tangent line**. The point of contact is the **point of tangency**.
Here is the foundational fact about tangent lines:
**A tangent line to a circle is perpendicular to the radius drawn to the point of tangency.**

blocks.title: Why must it be perpendicular?
blocks.body: Suppose the tangent at $T$ were *not* perpendicular to $OT$. Drop a perpendicular from $O$ to the tangent line, meeting it at some other point $T'$. Then $OT' < OT = r$ (the perpendicular is the shortest distance), so $T'$ would be inside the circle. But $T'$ is on the tangent line — meaning the tangent line enters the circle and would have to exit, intersecting the circle at two points. That contradicts the definition of tangent (touches at exactly one point). ∎

md: ### Practical consequences
**Right angle at the point of tangency.** Whenever a tangent and a radius meet, you have a $90°$ angle to work with — perfect for Pythagoras.

**Two tangents from an external point.** From any external point $E$ to a circle, exactly two tangent lines can be drawn. The two tangent lengths from $E$ (distance from $E$ to each point of tangency) are *equal*. We used this fact in the incircle lesson — tangent lengths from a vertex are equal.
### Worked example
A circle has center $O$ and radius $5$. From an external point $E$ at distance $13$ from $O$, draw a tangent. Find the tangent length (the distance from $E$ to the point of tangency $T$).
**Setup.** $\triangle OTE$ has a right angle at $T$ (tangent ⊥ radius). $OT = 5$ (radius), $OE = 13$ (given), $TE = ?$
**Pythagoras:** $TE^2 + OT^2 = OE^2 \Rightarrow TE^2 = 169 - 25 = 144 \Rightarrow TE = 12$.
(Yet another 5-12-13 triangle.)

blocks.title: Tangent-tangent angle theorem
blocks.body:
From external point $E$, draw two tangents touching the circle at $T_1$ and $T_2$. Then $ET_1 = ET_2$ (equal tangent lengths), and the angle $\angle T_1 E T_2$ at $E$ plus the central angle $\angle T_1 O T_2$ at $O$ sum to $180°$.

Intuitively: the quadrilateral $E T_1 O T_2$ has two right angles (at $T_1$ and $T_2$). The other two angles must complete the $360°$ — so they sum to $360 - 180 = 180°$.

md: ### Worked example (tangent from external point)
Two tangents are drawn from external point $E$ to a circle, touching at $T_1$ and $T_2$. The angle at $E$ is $40°$. Find the central angle $\angle T_1 O T_2$.
**Use the supplement rule:** $\angle T_1 O T_2 = 180° - \angle T_1 E T_2 = 180° - 40° = 140°$.
### Where this comes up
**Geometric constructions** — tangent from an external point (using a Thales-circle trick).
**Optimization** — "shortest path from a point to a circle" goes through the tangent.
**Physics** — instantaneous direction of a particle moving along a circle is tangent (perpendicular to the radius vector).
**Engineering** — gear teeth, belt drives, optics. Whenever something rolls or pivots around a fixed center.
